walkthrough.title: Unfold the Game Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get Clank set up. First, unfold the game board and place it in the center of the table. The board is double-sided, so pick whichever side your group wants to play. One side is a bit more straightforward and the other has trickier paths. For your first game, use the side that says it is recommended for beginners.

walkthrough.title: Place the Dragon Marker
walkthrough.text: Find the dragon marker and place it on the Rage Track on the board. It starts on the leftmost space of the track. The dragon gets angrier as players grab artifacts, so this marker will move to the right during the game. The further right it goes, the more cubes get pulled from the Dragon Bag during attacks.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Dragon Bag
walkthrough.text: Now grab the black cloth bag. This is the Dragon Bag, and it is one of the most important components in the game. Put twenty-four black cubes into the bag. These black cubes are safe cubes. When they get drawn during a dragon attack, nobody takes damage. Leave the bag in a spot everyone can reach.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Dungeon Row
walkthrough.text: Find the Dungeon Deck. These are the cards with a blue, purple, or red banner at the top. Give the deck a good shuffle and place it face down near the board. Then flip six cards face up in a row next to the deck. This is the Dungeon Row, and it is where you will buy new cards for your deck during the game.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Reserve Cards
walkthrough.text: Find three piles of reserve cards: Mercenary, Explore, and Secret Tome. Place them face up in their own stacks near the Dungeon Row. These are always available to purchase throughout the game. They never run out, so you can always fall back on them if the Dungeon Row does not have what you want.

walkthrough.title: Place Tokens on the Board
walkthrough.text: Now scatter the tokens onto the board. Place the artifact tokens on the marked artifact spaces deep in the dungeon. Place the minor secrets face down on their marked spaces. Put monkey idol tokens on the monkey idol spaces. Finally, place the market items on the market spaces near the top of the board. These include keys, backpacks, and crowns.

walkthrough.title: Choose Player Colors
walkthrough.text: Each player picks a color and takes the thirty matching cubes of that color. These are your Clank cubes, and they represent the noise you make in the dungeon. You will also take the matching pawn. Place your pawn on the start space at the top of the board, right at the dungeon entrance.

walkthrough.title: Build Your Starting Deck
walkthrough.text: Each player builds a starting deck of ten cards. Find six Burgle cards, two Stumble cards, one Cautious Advance card, and one Skillful Move card for each player. Every player starts with the exact same deck. Shuffle your ten cards and place them face down in front of you as your personal draw pile.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Health Trackers
walkthrough.text: Each player starts with ten health. Place your health marker on the ten space of your player area or use the health track on the board. When you take damage from dragon attacks, you will move this marker down. If it ever reaches zero, you are knocked out.

walkthrough.title: Draw Your First Hand
walkthrough.text: Each player draws five cards from the top of their personal draw pile. This is your starting hand. Keep your cards hidden from other players. On your turn, you will play all five cards, use their resources, then discard them and draw five fresh cards next turn.

walkthrough.title: Determine First Player
walkthrough.text: Pick a starting player however you like. Play will go clockwise from that player. The starting player does not get any special advantage, so choose however your group prefers. Once everyone has their five cards in hand and the board is set, you are ready to explore the dungeon. Let's learn the rules.

summary.title: Unfold the Game Board
bullets: Place double-sided board centrally — use beginner side for first game
Board shows dungeon paths, rooms, artifact spaces, and market

summary.title: Place the Dragon Marker
bullets: Place dragon marker on leftmost Rage Track space
Marker advances right as artifacts are taken → more cubes drawn per attack

summary.title: Prepare the Dragon Bag
bullets: Place 24 black cubes in the cloth Dragon Bag
Black cubes = safe, player-color cubes = damage

summary.title: Set Up the Dungeon Row
bullets: Shuffle Dungeon Deck → place face-down near board
Reveal 6 face-up cards as the Dungeon Row

summary.title: Set Up the Reserve Cards
bullets: Place Mercenary, Explore, and Secret Tome stacks face-up
Always available for purchase — never run out

summary.title: Place Tokens on the Board
bullets: Artifacts on artifact spaces deep in dungeon
Minor secrets face-down on secret spaces
Monkey idols on idol spaces, market items on market spaces

summary.title: Choose Player Colors
bullets: Each player takes 30 cubes of one color + matching pawn
Place pawn on start space at dungeon entrance

summary.title: Build Your Starting Deck
bullets: 10 cards each: 6 Burgle, 2 Stumble, 1 Cautious Advance, 1 Skillful Move
All players start with identical decks — shuffle and place face-down

summary.title: Set Up Health Trackers
bullets: Each player starts at 10 health
Dragon attacks reduce health — 0 health = knocked out

summary.title: Draw Your First Hand
bullets: Draw 5 cards from your personal draw pile
Play all 5 each turn → discard → draw 5 next turn

summary.title: Determine First Player
bullets: Choose starting player randomly — play clockwise
No first-player advantage — pick however you like

walkthrough.title: Goal of the Game
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about what you are trying to do in Clank. You are a thief sneaking into a dragon's dungeon to steal treasure. Your goal is to grab an artifact, then escape the dungeon alive. The player with the most victory points at the end wins. But here is the catch: if you get knocked out in the Depths of the dungeon, you score zero points no matter what you collected.

walkthrough.title: Turn Structure Overview
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you play all five cards from your hand. You do not choose which ones to play. Every card gets played, even the bad ones like Stumble. Your cards generate three types of resources: Skill, which is the blue diamond icon used to buy new cards. Swords, which are used to fight monsters. And Boots, which let you move your pawn through the dungeon. You spend these resources in any order you like during your turn.

walkthrough.title: The Clank Mechanic
walkthrough.text: Some cards have a Clank symbol on them. When you play a card with Clank, you must take that many of your colored cubes from your personal supply and place them in the Clank Area on the board. This represents the noise you are making in the dungeon. The more cubes you have in the Clank Area, the more likely you are to take damage during a dragon attack. Two of your starting cards, the Stumble cards, each generate one Clank.

walkthrough.title: Spending Skill to Buy Cards
walkthrough.text: Use your Skill points to buy cards from the Dungeon Row or the Reserve piles. Each card has a cost in the lower right corner. Blue-bannered cards go into your discard pile and will be shuffled into your deck later. Purple-bannered cards are devices that go to your play area and give you a permanent ability. When you buy a card from the Dungeon Row, immediately replace it with a new card from the Dungeon Deck.

walkthrough.title: Fighting Monsters
walkthrough.text: Red-bannered cards in the Dungeon Row are monsters. You do not buy monsters. Instead, you spend Swords equal to their strength to defeat them. When you defeat a monster, you gain the reward printed on the card, then the monster goes to a discard pile and a new card replaces it in the Row. Monster rewards can include gold, cards, or other benefits.

walkthrough.title: Movement and Boots
walkthrough.text: Spend Boots to move your pawn through the dungeon. Each tunnel between rooms costs one Boot. Some tunnels have special requirements. Tunnels with a sword icon require you to also spend one Sword to pass through. Locked tunnels require a key token. One-way tunnels can only be traveled in the direction of the arrow. Plan your route carefully because movement is often your scarcest resource.

walkthrough.title: Room Effects
walkthrough.text: When you enter a room, you may collect whatever is there. Minor secret tokens are picked up and kept face down until scoring. Monkey idol tokens are collected for set-based scoring. Market rooms let you buy items with gold. Some rooms have crystal cave icons that give you extra cards to draw. You only collect a room's contents the first time you enter it.

walkthrough.title: Market Items
walkthrough.text: When you reach a market space, you can spend gold to buy items. Keys cost seven gold and let you pass through locked tunnels. Backpacks give you extra carrying capacity. Crowns are worth victory points, usually eight to ten points each. Gold is earned from cards you play and from some room effects. You can buy market items anytime you are on a market space during your turn.

walkthrough.title: Picking Up Artifacts
walkthrough.text: Artifacts are the whole reason you are in the dungeon. Each artifact has a point value ranging from five to thirty victory points. The deeper you go, the more valuable the artifacts. When you pick up an artifact, the dragon gets angry. Advance the dragon marker on the Rage Track and immediately trigger a dragon attack. You can only carry one artifact at a time.

walkthrough.title: Dragon Attacks
walkthrough.text: Dragon attacks happen when certain cards appear in the Dungeon Row or when an artifact is taken. During an attack, first dump all cubes from the Clank Area into the Dragon Bag. Then draw cubes from the bag one at a time. The number drawn depends on the dragon's position on the Rage Track. Black cubes are safe and go back on the board. If a cube of your color is drawn, you take one damage and reduce your health by one.

walkthrough.title: Dragon Rage Escalation
walkthrough.text: As more artifacts are taken, the dragon marker moves further along the Rage Track. Early in the game, only two or three cubes might be drawn per attack. By the end, five or six cubes get drawn per attack. This creates a ticking clock. The longer you stay in the dungeon, the more dangerous it becomes. Every artifact taken by any player makes attacks worse for everyone.

walkthrough.title: Getting Knocked Out
walkthrough.text: If your health drops to zero, you are knocked out. Here is the critical rule: location matters. If you are knocked out in the Depths, which is the lower section of the board below a dividing line, you score zero points. Everything you collected is worthless. If you are knocked out above the Depths, a villager rescues you and you still get to score your treasure. This is what makes the push-your-luck tension so thrilling.

walkthrough.title: Escaping the Dungeon
walkthrough.text: To escape safely, you need to move your pawn all the way back to the start space or any above-ground space after picking up an artifact. You must have an artifact to escape. Once you escape, you are safe and fully score all your loot. You also trigger the endgame countdown. The first player to escape puts pressure on everyone still underground.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Countdown
walkthrough.text: When a player escapes or is knocked out, the endgame begins. Use the countdown track. On each subsequent turn, an automatic dragon attack happens. Players still in the dungeon must hurry to escape or at least get above the Depths. After the countdown ends, any players still in the dungeon are knocked out wherever they stand. If that is in the Depths, they score zero.

walkthrough.title: Deck Management Basics
walkthrough.text: When your draw pile runs out of cards, shuffle your discard pile to form a new draw pile. Every card you buy goes to your discard first and cycles into your deck later. This means new cards take a few turns to show up in your hand. Keep your deck lean and focused. Buying too many cards means your good cards get buried among mediocre ones.

walkthrough.title: End-of-Turn Cleanup
walkthrough.text: After you have spent all the resources you want and finished moving, your turn is over. Discard all five cards from your play area plus any cards you bought this turn into your personal discard pile. Then draw five new cards from your draw pile. If your draw pile has fewer than five cards, draw what is there, shuffle your discard to make a new draw pile, then draw the rest.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: Once the game ends, anyone who escaped or was knocked out above the Depths scores their loot. Add up your artifact value, gold coins, crown tokens, monkey idol set bonuses, minor secret tokens, and any victory points printed on cards in your deck. The player with the highest total wins. If there is a tie, the player who escaped first wins the tiebreaker.

bullets: Steal an artifact from the dragon's dungeon and escape alive
Highest VP total wins
Knocked out in the Depths = score 0

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Dungeon
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first game of Clank! In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted sequence with four players. Player 1, Player 2, Player 3, and Player 4 will each take different approaches to exploring the dungeon. Player 3 will make some common mistakes so you can learn what to avoid. Follow along even if you have fewer players at your table.

walkthrough.title: Starting Position
walkthrough.text: Everyone starts with the same ten-card deck and five cards in hand. All four pawns are on the start space at the dungeon entrance. The Dragon Bag has twenty-four black cubes in it and no player cubes yet. The Dungeon Row has six face-up cards ready for purchase. Let's begin.

walkthrough.title: Player 1's First Turn
walkthrough.text: Player 1 plays their five cards and gets two Skill, one Boot, and one Clank from a Stumble card. They place one of their colored cubes in the Clank Area. Then they spend the two Skill to buy an Explore card from the Reserve for three Skill. Wait, they only have two. They cannot afford Explore, so instead they use the one Boot to move one room into the dungeon. They save their remaining Skill for next turn. Actually, you cannot save resources between turns. Unused resources are lost, so Player 1 should have bought a card they could afford.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Spend Everything Each Turn
walkthrough.text: Here is the first important lesson. You cannot bank resources between turns. Every Skill, Sword, and Boot you generate must be used this turn or they disappear. Always look for ways to spend every point of every resource. Even a one-Skill Mercenary purchase is better than wasting Skill.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's First Turn
walkthrough.text: Player 2 plays their cards and gets three Skill and one Boot. They use the Boot to move one room into the dungeon, then spend three Skill to buy a useful card from the Dungeon Row. It is a card that provides two Boots and no Clank. Nice pickup. More Boots means faster movement through the dungeon. The card goes to their discard pile and will show up in a future hand.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's First Turn: Noisy Start
walkthrough.text: Player 3 plays their cards and gets two Skill and one Clank from Stumble. They see a powerful card in the Dungeon Row that costs four Skill and generates two Clank. They cannot afford it yet, so they buy a cheaper card that also generates one Clank. They now have two of their colored cubes in the Clank Area after just one turn. That is a lot of noise this early.

walkthrough.title: Why Early Clank Is Dangerous
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about why Player 3 should be worried. Right now, the Dragon Bag has twenty-four black cubes and only two of Player 3's cubes. That sounds safe, but those cubes accumulate. Every time a dragon attack happens, all cubes in the Clank Area get dumped into the bag. Player 3 is building up a stockpile of their cubes that will haunt them all game long.

walkthrough.title: Player 4's First Turn
walkthrough.text: Player 4 plays their cards and gets two Skill, one Sword, and one Boot. They move one room into the dungeon with the Boot. They spot a monster in the Dungeon Row that requires one Sword to defeat and gives a gold coin as a reward. They spend the Sword to defeat it, collect two gold, and spend their two Skill on a card that gives Boots. Smart play. Player 4 is building movement and keeping their Clank low.

walkthrough.title: A Few Turns Later
walkthrough.text: Let's fast forward a few turns. Players have been buying cards, moving deeper into the dungeon, and the Clank Area has been filling up. A dragon attack card appears in the Dungeon Row. Time for the first dragon attack. All cubes in the Clank Area get dumped into the Dragon Bag, and then cubes are drawn based on the Rage Track position. Three cubes come out. Two are black and one is Player 3's color. Player 3 takes one damage.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Manage Your Clank
walkthrough.text: That first damage to Player 3 is a wake-up call. Player 3 has been buying flashy high-Clank cards without thinking about the consequences. Meanwhile, Players 2 and 4 have been buying efficient low-Clank cards and barely have any cubes in the bag. The lesson is clear: every Clank cube you generate stays in the bag for the rest of the game. Be intentional about when and why you make noise.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Mistake: Diving Too Deep
walkthrough.text: Player 3 decides to push deep into the Depths to grab a thirty-point artifact. The problem is their deck has very few Boot cards. They can barely move two spaces per turn. Getting down to the artifact will take three or four turns, and getting back out will take even more. Meanwhile, the dragon is getting angrier with every artifact other players grab. Player 3 is about to get stuck.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Plan Your Exit Before Going Deep
walkthrough.text: This is one of the most common mistakes in Clank. Players fixate on the high-value artifacts deep in the Depths without counting how many turns it will take to get back out. Before you dive deep, count the rooms between your target and the exit. Make sure your deck has enough Boot cards to cover the return trip. A thirty-point artifact is worth zero if you get knocked out in the Depths.

walkthrough.title: Player 1 Grabs an Artifact
walkthrough.text: Player 1 has been moving steadily and reaches a mid-depth artifact worth fifteen points. They pick it up. The dragon marker advances on the Rage Track, and an immediate dragon attack triggers. Four cubes are drawn this time. Everyone checks their damage. Now the pressure is on. Player 1 starts heading for the exit while they are still healthy.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Mistake: Deck Bloat
walkthrough.text: Player 3 has been buying every cheap card they can afford, thinking more cards equals more power. But now their deck has eighteen cards in it, and they can only draw five per turn. Their two good Boot cards are buried among twelve mediocre Skill cards and four Clank-generating cards. Player 3's turns are becoming weak and unfocused because they drew Stumbles and filler instead of their key cards.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Keep Your Deck Lean
walkthrough.text: Deck bloat is a trap that catches many new players. In Clank, you draw exactly five cards per turn no matter what. If your deck has twenty cards, any specific card only shows up in one out of four hands. Buy cards that are genuinely better than your starting cards. A focused twelve-card deck with strong cards will outperform a bloated twenty-card deck full of mediocre filler every time.

walkthrough.title: The Race to Escape
walkthrough.text: Player 1 is heading for the exit with their artifact. Player 2 grabs a twenty-point artifact and starts their escape. Player 4 snatches a nearby artifact and begins running. Player 3 is still deep in the Depths, two rooms away from their target artifact. The Rage Track is climbing fast, and dragon attacks are pulling five cubes per attack now.

walkthrough.title: Player 1 Escapes
walkthrough.text: Player 1 reaches the dungeon entrance and escapes. The endgame countdown begins. From now on, an automatic dragon attack happens at the start of each turn. The remaining players feel the pressure mounting. Player 4 is two rooms from the exit. Player 2 is four rooms away. Player 3 has finally grabbed their artifact but is deep in the Depths with six rooms to go.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Fate
walkthrough.text: The countdown dragon attacks are relentless. Player 3 has too many cubes in the bag from all their noisy cards. Three attacks in a row each pull at least one of Player 3's cubes. Their health drops to zero while still in the Depths. Player 3 is knocked out below the line and scores zero points. That thirty-point artifact, all that gold, all those cards — worthless.

walkthrough.title: Final Results
walkthrough.text: Player 1 escaped with fifteen points in artifacts plus gold and secrets for a solid score. Player 2 made it out with a twenty-point artifact and some crowns for the highest total. Player 4 escaped with a modest artifact but had bought several high-VP cards, finishing in a close second. Player 3 scored zero. The lesson is clear: survival beats greed.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations! You have just seen a full game of Clank play out. You watched Player 3 make the three classic mistakes: generating too much Clank early, diving too deep without enough movement, and bloating their deck with too many cards. Remember: manage your noise, plan your escape route, and keep your deck focused. You are ready to play.

walkthrough.title: Before You Delve
walkthrough.text:
Before you start your own dungeon run, here are three things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for strategy basics and common mistakes to avoid.

Second — reference the Appendix for quick definitions of game terms and card types.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

Follow along even with fewer players at your table

summary.title: Before You Delve
bullets: Check out General Tips → strategy basics for beginners
Reference the Appendix → quick definitions of game terms
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we talk strategy, here are the biggest mistakes new Clank players make. First, generating too much Clank early by buying noisy cards without considering the long-term consequences. Second, diving deep into the Depths without enough movement cards to get back out. Third, bloating your deck with too many mediocre cards so your good cards rarely show up. Fourth, ignoring the endgame timer and getting caught deep underground when someone escapes. Keep these in mind and you will avoid the most painful losses.

walkthrough.title: Deck Building Philosophy
walkthrough.text: In Clank, quality beats quantity every time. You draw exactly five cards per turn, so every card matters. Before buying a card, ask yourself: is this better than my worst card? Your starting deck has six Burgle cards that give one Skill each and two Stumble cards that give nothing but Clank. Any card you buy should be meaningfully better than a Burgle. If a card is only slightly better, skip it. A lean deck with eight great cards cycles faster than a bloated deck with fifteen okay cards.

walkthrough.title: Movement Is King
walkthrough.text: Boots are the most valuable resource in Clank. You need movement to reach artifacts, collect room tokens, visit markets, and most importantly, escape alive. Many new players undervalue Boot cards because they are not as flashy as big Skill or Sword cards. But the player who can move three or four spaces per turn controls the game. They get first pick of artifacts, reach the best market items, and escape before the countdown gets deadly. Prioritize cards that provide Boots, especially ones with zero Clank.

walkthrough.title: Clank Management
walkthrough.text: Think of Clank as a long-term investment in your own death. Every cube you place in the Clank Area will eventually end up in the Dragon Bag, and it stays there for the rest of the game. Early game, the bag is mostly black cubes, so a few of your cubes seem harmless. But by late game, the ratio shifts dramatically. If you have eight cubes in the bag and there are only ten black cubes left, every attack is terrifying. Buy high-Clank cards only when the benefit clearly outweighs the risk. Sometimes the best card in the Row is one that removes Clank.

walkthrough.title: Artifact Selection Strategy
walkthrough.text: Not every artifact is worth the risk. The deepest artifacts offer the most points, but reaching them and escaping requires a deck built for speed and survival. For your first few games, aim for mid-depth artifacts worth ten to twenty points. These give you a solid score without requiring a dangerous trek through the Depths. As you get more experienced, you will learn when your deck is strong enough to go deep. Always count the rooms to the exit before committing to an artifact.

walkthrough.title: Market Item Priorities
walkthrough.text: When you reach a market, spend your gold wisely. Keys are worth buying early if you see locked tunnels on your planned route, because they open shortcuts that save precious Boots. Crowns are the most efficient victory points you can buy. An eight-gold crown converts directly to eight or ten VP. Backpacks are situational but useful if you want to carry extra loot. Always calculate whether spending gold on market items is better than just keeping the gold, since gold counts toward your final score too.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Table
walkthrough.text: Clank is a race, and you need to pay attention to what other players are doing. If someone has a fast movement deck and is heading toward an artifact, the endgame countdown could start sooner than you expect. If multiple players are generating lots of Clank, the Dragon Bag will become deadly fast. Watch the Rage Track closely. When the third artifact is taken, attacks become very dangerous. Use other players' actions to calibrate your own risk level.

walkthrough.title: The Escape Decision
walkthrough.text: Knowing when to turn around is the hardest skill in Clank. The temptation to grab one more secret or visit one more market is strong, but every extra turn in the dungeon is another dragon attack you have to survive. A good rule of thumb: start heading for the exit the moment you pick up your artifact. Do not detour unless the reward is clearly worth the risk. Remember that escaping first triggers the countdown and puts pressure on everyone else. Sometimes the best play is getting out early with a modest score while your opponents scramble.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't generate excessive Clank early — cubes stay in the bag forever
Don't dive deep without enough Boot cards to escape
Don't bloat your deck — quality over quantity
Don't ignore the endgame timer — escapes happen faster than you think

summary.title: Deck Building Philosophy
bullets: Quality beats quantity — every card in your 5-card hand matters
Only buy cards meaningfully better than Burgle (1 Skill)
Lean 12-card deck cycles faster than bloated 20-card deck
Ask before buying: is this better than my worst card?

summary.title: Movement Is King
bullets: Boots = most valuable resource in Clank
Movement controls artifact access, market access, and escape timing
Prioritize Boot cards, especially with 0 Clank
3-4 Boots per turn lets you control the game's pace

summary.title: Clank Management
bullets: Every Clank cube stays in the Dragon Bag permanently
Early Clank seems safe but compounds into late-game danger
Buy high-Clank cards only when benefit clearly outweighs risk
Cards that remove Clank can be game-saving

summary.title: Artifact Selection Strategy
bullets: Mid-depth artifacts (10-20 VP) are safest for beginners
Deep artifacts need a deck built for speed + survival
Always count rooms to exit before committing
A 15-VP artifact you escape with beats a 30-VP artifact you die with

summary.title: Market Item Priorities
bullets: Keys: buy early if locked tunnels are on your route
Crowns: best gold-to-VP conversion (8-10 VP each)
Gold also counts as VP — only buy items worth more than the gold spent
Backpacks: situational, useful for extra loot

summary.title: Reading the Table
bullets: Watch opponents' movement speed — fast players trigger early endgame
Track total Clank in the bag — more cubes = deadlier attacks
3rd artifact taken → attacks become very dangerous
Calibrate your risk based on other players' progress

summary.title: The Escape Decision
bullets: Start heading for exit the moment you grab your artifact
Every extra turn = another dragon attack to survive
Escaping first pressures opponents with countdown attacks
A modest score you escape with beats a huge score you die with

walkthrough.title: The Speed Run Strategy
walkthrough.text: One of the strongest approaches in Clank is the speed run. The idea is simple: buy Boot-heavy cards, grab the nearest decent artifact, and escape before anyone else. You do not need the highest-value artifact to win. A player who escapes with a fifteen-point artifact, a crown, and some secrets while triggering the countdown can beat a player who grabbed a thirty-point artifact but took damage from five countdown attacks. Speed runners should prioritize cards that give two or more Boots with zero Clank.

walkthrough.title: Building the Speed Deck
walkthrough.text: For a speed run, your ideal deck is ten to twelve cards total. You want three or four cards that provide Boots, two or three that provide Skill for opportunistic buys, and as few Clank generators as possible. Skip the two Stumble cards mentally. They are dead weight you cannot remove. Every other card should directly contribute to movement or provide strong utility. Avoid buying cards just because they are cheap. A one-Skill card is barely better than Burgle and adds deck bloat.

walkthrough.title: The Deep Dive Strategy
walkthrough.text: The opposite of speed running is the deep dive. This strategy aims for the highest-value artifacts deep in the Depths, typically twenty-five or thirty points. The trade-off is enormous risk. To pull this off, you need a deck with exceptional movement, low Clank generation, and ideally some healing. You also need to start your escape before other players trigger the countdown. Deep divers should buy cards early that provide both Boots and Swords, since some deep tunnels require Swords to pass through.

walkthrough.title: Timing the Deep Dive
walkthrough.text: The critical question for deep divers is when to turn around. Count the rooms between your target artifact and the nearest exit above the Depths line. If that number is higher than your average Boots per turn, you are in trouble. A good deep diver starts their escape one or two turns before they think they need to. The Rage Track does not wait for you. If two other players have already taken artifacts, the attack frequency means you are on borrowed time.

walkthrough.title: The Market Mogul Strategy
walkthrough.text: Some players focus less on expensive artifacts and more on accumulating gold and buying crowns. Crowns are worth eight to ten victory points each and do not require you to go deep into the dungeon. A market mogul buys cards that generate gold, visits every market space they can reach, and purchases crowns and keys. Combined with a mid-value artifact and some secrets, this can produce a very competitive score without the risk of deep diving.

walkthrough.title: Card Synergy Combos
walkthrough.text: The best Clank decks have cards that work together. Look for combos where one card enables another. Cards that let you draw extra cards are incredibly powerful because they let you see more of your deck each turn, effectively breaking the five-card hand limit. Cards that give you benefits for having companions, or that reward you for playing cards of a certain type, create multiplier effects. When evaluating a card to buy, think about how it interacts with cards you already own, not just how strong it is in isolation.

walkthrough.title: Trash Talk: Removing Bad Cards
walkthrough.text: Some cards and effects let you remove cards from your deck permanently. This is one of the most powerful mechanics in any deck-building game. Removing a Stumble card does two things: it eliminates a source of Clank, and it makes your deck one card thinner so your good cards show up more often. If you see an opportunity to trash a Stumble or a Burgle, take it seriously. A nine-card deck with no dead weight is a precision instrument.

walkthrough.title: Dragon Bag Probability
walkthrough.text: Advanced players think about the Dragon Bag in terms of probability. Early game, the bag has twenty-four black cubes and very few player cubes, so the chance of getting hit is low. As the game progresses, black cubes get drawn out and player cubes accumulate. By late game, if there are twelve cubes in the bag and four are yours, you have roughly a one in three chance of being hit on each draw. With five draws per attack at high Rage, that is almost a guaranteed hit every attack. Use this math to decide when you are safe and when you need to run.

walkthrough.title: Monkey Idol Set Scoring
walkthrough.text: Monkey idols are a sneaky source of points that many players overlook. They score based on how many you collect: one idol is worth one point, two are worth four, three are worth nine, and so on. The scoring is exponential, so collecting a third or fourth idol is far more valuable than your first. If you see idol spaces along your route, plan to grab them. They can swing a close game by five or more points. Route your path through idol spaces whenever it does not cost too many extra Boots.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Pressure Tactics
walkthrough.text: If you have a fast deck and a decent artifact, escaping early is a weapon. The countdown attacks punish players who are still deep in the dungeon, especially those with lots of cubes in the bag. By escaping first, you are essentially attacking every other player each turn with dragon damage. This is especially devastating against deep divers who are four or five rooms from safety. Time your escape not just for your own safety, but to maximize pressure on your opponents. The best Clank players win not just by collecting the most treasure, but by making sure their opponents cannot keep theirs.

summary.title: The Speed Run Strategy
bullets: Buy Boot-heavy cards, grab nearest decent artifact, escape first
Speed runners trigger countdown and pressure opponents
15-VP artifact + safe escape often beats 30-VP artifact + knockout
Prioritize 2+ Boot cards with 0 Clank

summary.title: Building the Speed Deck
bullets: Target 10-12 total cards — lean and focused
3-4 Boot cards, 2-3 Skill cards, minimal Clank generators
Skip cards that are barely better than Burgle
Every card should directly contribute to movement or utility

summary.title: The Deep Dive Strategy
bullets: Target 25-30 VP artifacts deep in the Depths
Requires exceptional movement, low Clank, ideally healing
Buy cards with both Boots and Swords for deep tunnels
Enormous risk — must escape before countdown overwhelms you

summary.title: Timing the Deep Dive
bullets: Count rooms from target artifact to nearest above-Depths exit
If rooms > average Boots/turn, you're in trouble
Start escape 1-2 turns before you think you need to
2+ artifacts taken by others = you're on borrowed time

summary.title: The Market Mogul Strategy
bullets: Focus on gold generation and buying crowns (8-10 VP each)
Visit every reachable market space
Mid-value artifact + crowns + secrets = competitive score
Lower risk than deep diving — stay above the Depths

summary.title: Card Synergy Combos
bullets: Cards that draw extra cards break the 5-card hand limit
Companion cards and type-based bonuses create multipliers
Evaluate cards by how they interact with your existing deck
Synergy > raw power in isolation

summary.title: Trash Talk: Removing Bad Cards
bullets: Trashing cards = one of the most powerful deck-building mechanics
Removing Stumble: eliminates Clank + thins deck
Removing Burgle: makes each draw more impactful
9-card deck with no dead weight = precision instrument

summary.title: Dragon Bag Probability
bullets: Early game: 24 black + few player cubes = low hit chance
Late game: fewer black cubes + accumulated player cubes = deadly
4 of your cubes in 12 total = ~33% hit per draw
At 5 draws/attack, that's near-guaranteed damage every attack

summary.title: Monkey Idol Set Scoring
bullets: Scoring is exponential: 1=1, 2=4, 3=9 VP
3rd and 4th idols are far more valuable than the 1st
Route through idol spaces when possible
Can swing close games by 5+ points

summary.title: Endgame Pressure Tactics
bullets: Escaping first = weaponizing countdown attacks against opponents
Especially devastating against deep divers far from exit
Time your escape to maximize pressure, not just safety
Best players win by ensuring opponents can't keep their treasure

entries.definition: Starting card. Provides one Skill point for buying cards. Six copies in every player's starting deck. The baseline card — any purchase should be better than this.
Starting card. Provides no resources and generates one Clank. Two copies in every starting deck. The worst card in the game — trash it if you get the chance.
Starting card. Provides one Boot for movement. One copy in every starting deck. Your only starting movement card besides Skillful Move.
Starting card. Provides one Boot and one Skill. One copy in every starting deck. The best card in your starting deck — provides both movement and buying power.
Resource shown as a blue diamond icon. Used to buy cards from the Dungeon Row or Reserve piles. Generated by playing cards on your turn. Unused Skill is lost at end of turn.
Resource shown as a sword icon. Used to fight monsters in the Dungeon Row and to pass through monster-guarded tunnels on the board. Unused Swords are lost at end of turn.

Resource shown as a boot icon. Used to move your pawn through dungeon tunnels. Each tunnel costs one Boot. The most critical resource for artifact retrieval and escape. Unused Boots are lost at end of turn.

Currency earned from cards and room effects. Used to buy market items like keys, backpacks, and crowns. Unlike Skill, Swords, and Boots, unspent gold carries over between turns and counts as victory points at end of game.

Noise generated by certain cards. When a card generates Clank, place that many of your colored cubes from your supply into the Clank Area on the board. These cubes get dumped into the Dragon Bag during attacks and can result in damage when drawn.
A designated space on the game board where players place their Clank cubes during their turns. When a dragon attack occurs, all cubes in the Clank Area are dumped into the Dragon Bag before drawing.

A cloth bag that starts with twenty-four black cubes. Player-colored cubes are added from the Clank Area during dragon attacks. Cubes are drawn from the bag to determine who takes damage. Black cubes are safe; player-colored cubes deal one damage to that player.

Triggered by dragon symbols on Dungeon Row cards or when an artifact is taken. All Clank Area cubes go into the Dragon Bag, then a number of cubes are drawn based on the Rage Track position. Player-colored cubes drawn deal one damage each.

A track on the board showing the dragon's anger level. The dragon marker starts at the leftmost position and advances each time an artifact is taken. Higher positions mean more cubes drawn during dragon attacks, making the game progressively more dangerous.

The lower section of the dungeon board, separated by a visible dividing line. Contains the most valuable artifacts but is the most dangerous area. If you are knocked out while in the Depths, you score zero points for the entire game.

When a player's health reaches zero. If knocked out in the Depths, that player scores zero. If knocked out above the Depths, a villager rescues the player and they still score their collected treasure. Getting knocked out does not end the game immediately.

Each player starts with ten health points. Damage from dragon attacks reduces health by one per player-colored cube drawn. Reaching zero health means being knocked out. Some cards can heal or prevent damage.

Six face-up cards from the Dungeon Deck, available for purchase or fighting. Cards have blue banners (deck cards), purple banners (devices), or red banners (monsters). When a card is acquired or defeated, it is immediately replaced from the deck.

Cards purchased from the Dungeon Row or Reserve that go into your discard pile. They will be shuffled into your draw pile when it runs out. These cards provide Skill, Swords, Boots, gold, and special abilities.

Special cards purchased from the Dungeon Row that go directly to your play area instead of your discard pile. They provide permanent abilities or one-time powerful effects. Devices do not cycle through your deck.

Cards in the Dungeon Row that cannot be bought. Instead, spend Swords equal to their strength to defeat them. Defeating monsters grants rewards like gold, cards, or other benefits. The monster is discarded and replaced.

Reserve card always available for purchase. Costs one Skill and provides two Swords. Useful when you need combat power but the Dungeon Row does not have what you want. Goes to your discard pile like a normal deck card.
Reserve card always available for purchase. Costs three Skill and provides two Boots and two Skill. A solid movement card that also helps with future purchases. Goes to your discard pile.

Reserve card always available for purchase. Costs seven Skill and is worth seven victory points at end of game. An expensive but guaranteed source of VP for Skill-heavy decks. Goes to your discard pile.

Treasure tokens placed on specific spaces deep in the dungeon. Each has a VP value from five to thirty. Taking an artifact advances the Rage Track and triggers a dragon attack. You must have an artifact to escape the dungeon. Only one artifact can be carried at a time.

Small tokens placed face-down on certain dungeon spaces. Picked up when you enter the room. Kept secret until end-of-game scoring, when they are revealed for various small bonuses like extra VP, gold, or healing.

Collectable tokens placed on specific dungeon spaces. Score based on set collection: one idol is worth one point, two idols are worth four points, three are worth nine, and so on. The scoring formula is the number of idols squared.
Market item costing seven gold. Allows passage through locked tunnels on the board. A key is consumed when used to unlock a tunnel. Keys can open valuable shortcuts that save movement.
Market item that provides extra carrying capacity. Allows you to pick up additional loot tokens from the dungeon. Useful for players collecting many minor secrets or monkey idols.
Market item worth eight to ten victory points. One of the best gold-to-VP conversions in the game. Purchased at market spaces on the board. Multiple crowns can be purchased if you have enough gold.

A path connecting two rooms on the board. Moving through a tunnel costs one Boot. Some tunnels have additional requirements: a sword icon means you must also spend one Sword, a lock icon means you need a key, and arrow icons indicate one-way travel.
Special room icon on certain dungeon spaces. When you enter a crystal cave, you draw extra cards from your personal draw pile, giving you more resources to work with on your current turn.

A tunnel marked with an arrow that can only be traveled in the direction the arrow points. These create strategic routing decisions and can trap unwary players who enter an area with no other way out.
A tunnel with a lock icon that requires a key token to pass through. Keys can be purchased at market spaces for seven gold. Locked tunnels often provide valuable shortcuts through the dungeon.

Your personal face-down deck of cards. Draw five cards from it at the end of each turn. When it runs out, shuffle your discard pile to form a new draw pile. New purchases enter through the discard pile.
Your personal pile of used cards. At end of turn, all played cards and newly purchased cards go here. When your draw pile is empty, shuffle the discard pile to create a new draw pile.

Permanently removing a card from your deck. Some card effects allow you to trash cards. Trashed cards go back to the box and will never be drawn again. Extremely valuable for removing Stumble and weak cards.

Triggered when the first player escapes the dungeon or is knocked out. An automatic dragon attack occurs each subsequent turn. After the countdown track ends, all players still in the dungeon are knocked out at their current position.

Moving your pawn to the dungeon entrance or any above-ground space while carrying an artifact. Escaping ends your participation in the game and triggers the endgame countdown if it has not started already. Escaped players fully score all collected treasure.

Add up artifact VP, gold coins, crown VP, monkey idol set VP, minor secret bonuses, and VP printed on cards in your deck. Only players who escaped or were knocked out above the Depths can score. Tiebreaker goes to the player who escaped first.
